# Project Charter

<!-- Generated by `spec-kitty charter generate` -->

Generated: 2026-04-27T05:31:18Z

## Testing Standards

- Target 80%+ test coverage on Go services. All test helpers must call t.Helper(). Use context-aware database methods. Per-service tests run via `task test:SERVICE`; full suite via `task test`. Distinguish test tiers: unit tests should be fast and may use well-scoped fakes or in-process test doubles for in-service logic; integration tests that verify behavior crossing service boundaries must run against real dependencies (Postgres, Elasticsearch) and use the CI integration harness. Use build tags to separate quick vs slow suites so local feedback remains fast while CI runs full integration checks. UI changes require manual browser verification — type-check and unit tests do not establish feature correctness.

## Quality Gates

- Required before merge: (1) golangci-lint clean via `task lint:force` (matches CI; no os.Getenv outside cmd/ and infrastructure/config/, cognitive complexity ≤20, funlen ≤100, line length ≤150, no interface{}, named constants for magic numbers, pre-allocated slices, no variable shadowing); (2) `task test` for changed services; (3) `task drift:check` clean (specs aligned with code); (4) `task ports:check` clean after compose changes; (5) `task layers:check` clean (no upward layer imports); (6) lefthook pre-commit and pre-push hooks pass without --no-verify. CI must be green before merge.

## Modern Go 1.26+ Enforcement

- Language rules: Go 1.26+ idioms only. Forbidden — untyped nil returns; global vars except immutable constants; `init()` functions except for pure constant registration; reflection for runtime behavior (test-only). Required — prefer immutable structs (copy-by-value) unless mutation is needed; avoid pointer receivers unless mutation or large-struct copy cost; preallocate slices and maps when sizes are predictable.
- Framework rules: Dependency injection is constructor-first — no service locators. No "god packages" and no `utils` packages — name packages by domain with a single responsibility. No panics outside truly unrecoverable bootstrap paths (issue #700 tracks remaining offenders). No magic strings — use typed enums (`iota` typed enums or custom string types). No YAML-driven runtime behavior — config is typed, validated, and explicit at load time.
- Architecture rules: Every service is a closed world — no leaking types across boundaries. Every handler is thin orchestration with business logic in domain packages. Repositories are interface-driven only where mocking is required. Interfaces live in the consumer package, not the provider. No circular dependencies. No new abstractions during god-file split missions — move code only. Every WP that changes public behavior carries a contract test.

## Performance Benchmarks

- No new pipeline-level performance targets are set as part of this charter. Existing service-level expectations stand: classifier and publisher must keep up with crawler throughput; search API responses typically under 2s. Performance regressions surfaced by the drift governor or production observability are treated as bugs and triaged into the backlog.

## Branch Strategy

- Solo maintainer. No human peer reviewer exists. Review is performed by a Spec Kitty implement-review loop where an agent acts as reviewer and the maintainer arbitrates rejections. CI gates (lint, tests, drift, layer-check, ports SSOT) are non-negotiable backstops. For any change the agent reviewer cannot confidently approve, the maintainer reviews directly with a fresh-eyes pass before merge. Force-pushes to main are forbidden.
- Deployment constraints: Production runs on Docker Compose orchestrated by a deploy.sh script invoked by GitHub Actions; production is NOT a git checkout. Migrations must use unique numeric prefixes (golang-migrate crashes on duplicates). ES mapping changes go through index-manager. Container naming follows `north-cloud-{service}`. Squid, nginx, Caddy, and Redis production configs have known gotchas documented in CLAUDE.md and must be respected. Health checks plus auto-rollback gate every deploy. Dev uses docker-compose.base.yml + docker-compose.dev.yml.

## Governance Activation

```yaml
mission: software-dev
selected_paradigms: []
selected_directives: []
available_tools: [git, spec-kitty]
template_set: software-dev-default
```

## Policy Summary

- Intent: North Cloud is a content intelligence pipeline (crawler → classifier + ML sidecars → publisher routing → Redis pub/sub → consumers). The repo is treated as frozen except for the open issue backlog. Goal: triage that backlog, then implement the surviving items cleanly using modern Go and industry best practices, returning the repo to a pristine state. Spec Kitty drives the workflow. The first mission is a backlog triage that deprecates, groups, and prioritizes the 13 open issues; each survivor (or cluster) becomes its own spec-driven mission. Trivial fixes may bypass the full loop; non-trivial work goes through specify → plan → tasks → implement → review.
- Languages/Frameworks: Go 1.26+ for all backend services (monorepo with per-service modules, GOWORK=off, go.work for IDE only). Vue 3 + TypeScript + Vite for the dashboard frontend. Python is reserved for ML sidecars under ml-sidecars/ and ml-modules/. PHP, other languages, or new frameworks are out of scope without an amendment. Shared code lives only under infrastructure/; services must not import from each other. Scaffold- level dependencies (Redis, Postgres, Elasticsearch, Docker Compose, Caddy, nginx) are fixed by the existing architecture and not up for debate inside a mission.
- Testing: Target 80%+ test coverage on Go services. All test helpers must call t.Helper(). Use context-aware database methods. Per-service tests run via `task test:SERVICE`; full suite via `task test`. Distinguish test tiers: unit tests should be fast and may use well-scoped fakes or in-process test doubles for in-service logic; integration tests that verify behavior crossing service boundaries must run against real dependencies (Postgres, Elasticsearch) and use the CI integration harness. Use build tags to separate quick vs slow suites so local feedback remains fast while CI runs full integration checks. UI changes require manual browser verification — type-check and unit tests do not establish feature correctness.
- Quality Gates: Required before merge: (1) golangci-lint clean via `task lint:force` (matches CI; no os.Getenv outside cmd/ and infrastructure/config/, cognitive complexity ≤20, funlen ≤100, line length ≤150, no interface{}, named constants for magic numbers, pre-allocated slices, no variable shadowing); (2) `task test` for changed services; (3) `task drift:check` clean (specs aligned with code); (4) `task ports:check` clean after compose changes; (5) `task layers:check` clean (no upward layer imports); (6) lefthook pre-commit and pre-push hooks pass without --no-verify. CI must be green before merge.
- Review Policy: Solo maintainer. No human peer reviewer exists. Review is performed by a Spec Kitty implement-review loop where an agent acts as reviewer and the maintainer arbitrates rejections. CI gates (lint, tests, drift, layer-check, ports SSOT) are non-negotiable backstops. For any change the agent reviewer cannot confidently approve, the maintainer reviews directly with a fresh-eyes pass before merge. Force-pushes to main are forbidden.
- Performance Targets: No new pipeline-level performance targets are set as part of this charter. Existing service-level expectations stand: classifier and publisher must keep up with crawler throughput; search API responses typically under 2s. Performance regressions surfaced by the drift governor or production observability are treated as bugs and triaged into the backlog.
- Deployment Constraints: Production runs on Docker Compose orchestrated by a deploy.sh script invoked by GitHub Actions; production is NOT a git checkout. Migrations must use unique numeric prefixes (golang-migrate crashes on duplicates). ES mapping changes go through index-manager. Container naming follows `north-cloud-{service}`. Squid, nginx, Caddy, and Redis production configs have known gotchas documented in CLAUDE.md and must be respected. Health checks plus auto-rollback gate every deploy. Dev uses docker-compose.base.yml + docker-compose.dev.yml.

## Project Directives

1. Respect risk boundaries: NC is the content pipeline layer only. Out of scope: the entity model (Minoo), framework internals (Waaseyaa), editorial decisions, frontend rendering of consuming apps. Services import only from infrastructure/; no cross-service imports. The classifier must import indigenous taxonomy slugs from jonesrussell/indigenous-taxonomy rather than hardcoding them. No --no-verify, no --no-gpg-sign, no force-push to main, no skipping CI, no destructive git operations without explicit maintainer instruction. Secrets never committed.
2. Keep documentation synchronized with workflow and behavior changes.
3. Modern Go discipline (DIR-003, severity: error). All Go code must follow the rules in the "Modern Go 1.26+ Enforcement" section above. golangci-lint is law — 75 linters enabled, no new `nolint` directives without an inline comment justifying scope and sunset. Thresholds: funlen ≤100, cyclop ≤30, gocognit ≤20, lll ≤150, coverage ≥80% (refactors may not regress coverage). Forbidden: `interface{}` (use `any` or generics), `os.Getenv` outside `cmd/` and `infrastructure/config/`, untyped nil returns, global vars except immutable constants, `init()` except constant registration, reflection for behavior, service locators, god packages, `utils` packages, panics outside unrecoverable bootstrap, magic strings, YAML-driven runtime behavior, interface proliferation, cross-service imports.
4. Mission execution discipline (DIR-004, severity: error). One PR per work package — never bundle WPs across or within missions. Never refactor adjacent code while executing a mission ("while I'm here…" is the named anti-pattern). During god-file split missions: move code only — no new abstractions, packages, interfaces, or renames beyond what the linter requires. During scope-boundary decision missions: produce evidence and analysis only; the maintainer chooses the option. Spec Kitty ceremony order is mandatory: plan → tasks → tasks-packages → tasks-finalize → implement → review → accept. Pre-commit gates: `task lint`, `task test`, `task layers:check`, `task drift:check` all green; `--no-verify` is forbidden.

## Amendment Process

Charter amendments are proposed by editing .kittify/charter/interview/answers.yaml and regenerating the bundle, or by direct PR to charter.md with rationale. Maintainer self-approves amendments since there is no peer reviewer, but each amendment must state what changed and why in the commit message. Mission-scope deviations that do not amend the charter are recorded as exceptions on the mission, not silently absorbed.

## Exception Policy

Exceptions to quality gates (e.g. a one-off lint suppression, a coverage shortfall, a temporary spec drift) must be documented in the PR or mission notes with: (a) scope of the exception, (b) reason, (c) sunset criteria or follow-up issue. Exceptions are not granted for force-pushing main, skipping git hooks, committing secrets, or bypassing the layer/drift/ports checks.
